key_learnings: Supplements should complement — not replace — a nutrient-dense whole-food diet. Evidence quality varies enormously across supplement categories; very few have robust RCT support for meaningful clinical outcomes. Bioavailability of supplement forms differs significantly (e.g., magnesium glycinate versus oxide; methylcobalamin versus cyanocobalamin for B12).
research_findings: Vitamin D3 combined with K2 supplementation is more effective for bone health than D3 alone, as K2 ensures calcium is directed to bone rather than arterial tissue. Magnesium deficiency affects up to 50% of adults and is frequently underdiagnosed because standard serum testing reflects only 1% of total body magnesium. Omega-3 fish oil supplementation at 2–4g EPA+DHA per day reduces triglycerides by 20–30% and is FDA-approved for hypertriglyceridemia management.
current_focus_areas: Building an evidence-tiered supplement recommendation engine (Tier 1: strong RCT evidence; Tier 2: observational or mechanistic; Tier 3: emerging or preliminary); developing contraindication and drug-nutrient interaction screening tools; integrating with Bot 5 (Micronutrient Deficiency Detection) to prioritize supplement recommendations based on detected deficiencies.

learning_log.entry: Assigned to the Nutritional Assessment & Genomics department under Department Leader Bot 5 (Micronutrient Deficiency Detection). Continuing independent supplement research and reporting daily to Copilot. Progress: 43%.
